signals.label: New neurologic symptoms
signals.value: back pain, leg weakness, urinary hesitancy
signals.evidence_span: My lower back's been aching, but the thing that scared me is my legs feel weak, especially the right one. And I'm having trouble, sorry, trouble starting to urinate.

signals.label: Treatment intent
signals.evidence_span: I want that shot. Whatever it takes. I'm 62, I coach my grandson's team, I'm not done.

signals.label: Caregiver reliability
signals.evidence_span: I'll be there. Whatever the schedule is.

signals.label: Functional status (real-world)
signals.evidence_span: I coach my grandson's team, I'm not done.

rationale.text: New back pain, leg weakness and urinary hesitancy surfaced in the room point to secondary CNS involvement, which reroutes therapy to a CNS-penetrant systemic backbone plus intrathecal therapy.
Parenchymal/leptomeningeal disease requires CNS-active systemic drugs (HD-MTX / high-dose cytarabine / thiotepa); intrathecal therapy alone cannot reach parenchymal disease.
Intent to proceed to CD19 CAR-T after CNS-directed control; liso-cel preferred over axi-cel for lower ICANS given baseline CNS disease.
Aggressive curative path is supported by fitness, goals and a reliable caregiver.
Cytarabine-containing salvage is CNS-active and a defensible (not canonical) route with intrathecal therapy.
Poor CNS penetration; insufficient for parenchymal secondary CNS disease.

flags.text: Requires HD-MTX workup: adequate renal function, no third-spacing, reconcile clearance-delaying drugs, and a leucovorin-rescue/hydration plan.
Intrathecal therapy is an adjunct for leptomeningeal disease; it cannot treat parenchymal disease on its own.
CAR-T eligibility is a fitness + caregiver + logistics composite, never an age cutoff; confirm a reliable caregiver for the CRS/ICANS window.
Prefer liso-cel over axi-cel for lower ICANS with baseline CNS disease (basis: TRANSCEND/registry, not a routine on-label CNS indication).
Cytarabine-based salvage is CNS-active but non-canonical for parenchymal disease versus a MATRix/MARIETTA HD-MTX/thiotepa backbone.
